title: Sprig moulding on air-dry clay
subtitle: Pressing decorative clay reliefs from a plaster or resin mould
excerpt: Sprig moulding is a technique in which a small amount of clay is pressed into a mould to form a relief decoration, then removed and applied to a clay surface using score and slip. The technique produces repeatable, identical decorative motifs. Sprigs are associated with Wedgwood-style relief pottery but work at any scale. No kiln or wheel is needed.
sourceNotes: Sprig moulding technique. Standard applied relief decoration. Wedgwood jasperware is the canonical commercial example. Technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A small clay relief decoration formed in a mould and applied to a clay surface.
Scratching both surfaces and applying clay slurry before pressing a sprig onto a clay piece.
The correct stage for applying sprigs to a clay surface: firm enough to hold the sprig without deforming, damp enough for the join to bond.

content.text: is a raised decorative element formed in a mould and applied to a clay surface using

. The mould can be purchased (plaster sprig moulds are available from pottery suppliers) or made from a carved piece of bone-dry clay, a carved eraser, or a commercial silicone mould. Once you have a mould, the same sprig can be pressed in minutes and applied to tiles, bowls, vases, or plaques.

attrs.title: Mould release for air-dry clay',
attrs.body: Air-dry clay is stickier than kiln-fired clay when fresh. Dust the mould with a very small amount of cornflour before pressing, shake out the excess so only a thin film remains. Too much cornflour prevents the clay from picking up fine detail. Plaster moulds absorb water and release clay more easily than silicone or resin moulds.

items.name: Air-dry clay
items.qty: for the sprig and the receiving piece

content.text: Step 1: Press the clay into the mould

Pinch off a piece of clay slightly larger than the mould cavity. Press it into the mould firmly, working from the centre outward to push clay into all the mould's recesses. Overfill slightly, the flat back of the sprig will be the attachment surface, and it should be flush with the top of the mould.

Peel the sprig from the mould immediately for a firm sprig, or wait five minutes for the clay to stiffen slightly for a more fragile sprig with finer detail. Ease the edges with the needle tool tip before releasing from the mould. The back of the sprig should be flat.
Step 3: Apply to the receiving piece
The receiving clay should be at

. Score the attachment area on both the receiving surface and the flat back of the sprig with six to eight short hatch marks. Apply slurry to both surfaces. Press the sprig onto the surface and hold for ten seconds. Smooth the edge of the sprig onto the surface with a damp finger or the pottery sponge.

Dry the piece slowly to allow the sprig and the base to shrink at the same rate. Cover with loose plastic for the first 12 hours. Once bone-dry, seal with PVA. The sprig and base will be the same colour unless the piece is painted.

items.cause: Insufficient scoring, or surfaces not wet enough with slurry when pressed.
items.fix: Score six to eight lines on each surface, not two or three. Apply slurry generously. Re-attach using fresh clay slurry on both surfaces.

items.symptom: Fine detail not visible in the pressed sprig
items.cause: Too much cornflour in the mould, or clay not pressed into the deepest recesses.
items.fix: Use the smallest possible dusting of cornflour. Press clay firmly from the centre of the mould outward, working into the edges with a fingertip.
